metadata.summary: PMBOK 8 replaces 10 Knowledge Areas with seven performance domains for value delivery, systems thinking, and sustainability integration.

content.title: Seven Performance Domains (PMBOK 8)
content.summary: PMBOK 8 replaces the 10 Knowledge Areas with seven performance domains — Governance, Scope, Schedule, Finance, Stakeholders, Resources, Risk — as the primary PM rubric. Quality, Communications, and Procurement are absorbed into these domains rather than standing alone. The framework shifts emphasis from process compliance to value delivery, systems thinking, sustainability, and governance integration.

why.p: Domain-based framing produces more actionable health checks than process-group-based framing: each domain maps to one accountable person and produces a measurable RAG status rather than a checklist of artifacts. Agents can score a project against seven explicit axes and surface cross-domain cause/effect chains (e.g., Resources Red → Schedule Amber) that process-group audits routinely miss.

list.item: Greenfield PMO design where you'd otherwise default to PMBOK 6 process groups.
Project audits and health checks — domains give a structured rubric (one section per domain).
Tailoring conversations: "which domains apply to this project, with what intensity?"
PM certification alignment (PMP exam content since 2023+ leans on these domains).
Coaching new PMs to think in outcomes rather than artifact production.
Deep technical execution decisions — domains are PM-level abstractions, not engineering rubrics.
Pure agile teams already running Scrum + product OKRs without a separate domain map.
Teams committed to PMBOK 6 under a regulator's mandate — switching costs more than it returns.
Day-to-day sprint operations — domains live at portfolio/program tempo, not stand-up tempo.

section.name: domain-map
section.p: Seven domains replace the ten Knowledge Areas. Quality, Communications, and Procurement are absorbed — not removed — into the remaining domains. All domains operate concurrently; none is sequential to another.

rule.statement: Assign one accountable domain owner per domain on every project, even when one person holds multiple domains.
rule.rationale: Domains with no named owner drift to green in status reports regardless of actual health; accountability produces honest reporting.

section.name: governance
section.p: Replaces Integration Management. Covers oversight, decision frameworks, and value realization tracking.

list.item: Establish project authority and decision rights
Link project outcomes to portfolio value
Manage change control and escalation paths

section.name: scope
section.p: Enhanced definition over PMBOK 6 Scope Management. Requires explicit documented scope boundaries, not just a scope statement.

list.item: Define and baseline what is in scope and out of scope
Control scope changes through the governance domain
Validate deliverables against acceptance criteria

section.name: schedule
section.p: Same focus as PMBOK 6 Time Management. Covers plan, sequence, estimate, and control timelines and dependencies.

list.item: Identify all activities and dependencies
Develop schedule with critical path and resource constraints
Monitor schedule performance and forecast completion

section.name: finance
section.p: Replaces Cost Management. Broader financial lens including ROI, benefits realization, and financial risk.

list.item: Develop and baseline budget
Track actual vs. planned expenditure (EVM optional)
Forecast financial performance and report to sponsors

section.name: stakeholders
section.p: Now absorbs Communications. Single domain for identifying, analyzing, engaging, and communicating with all stakeholders.

list.item: Build and maintain stakeholder register
Develop engagement strategies per stakeholder segment
Plan and execute communications — frequency, channel, tone

antipattern.description: Treating Communications as automatic once it is absorbed into Stakeholders.
antipattern.reason: Without an explicit comms plan, communications gets under-invested and stakeholder satisfaction drops regardless of delivery quality.

section.name: resources
section.p: Enhanced focus on both human and physical resources. Covers acquisition, development, and management.

list.item: Acquire team members and physical resources
Develop team capability (see team-development methodology)
Manage resource conflicts and availability

section.name: risk
section.p: Same focus as PMBOK 6 Risk Management. Covers identify, analyze, respond to, and monitor uncertainties.

list.item: Identify risks and opportunities systematically
Analyze probability and impact
Implement responses and monitor risk register

section.name: new-emphases-in-pmbok-8
section.p: Four critical shifts define PMBOK 8 philosophy:

list.item: Value Delivery over output delivery — outputs shipped vs. outcomes realized tracked separately
Systems Thinking — understand project in broader organizational context
Sustainability — environmental, social, and economic impact; requires measurable metrics
Governance Integration — stronger portfolio/program/project linkage

rule.statement: Track value realization rate (outcomes realized / outputs shipped) alongside delivery metrics.
rule.rationale: A 100% delivery rate with 30% value realization rate is the most common trap; domains must surface it explicitly.

rule.statement: Anchor every domain color rating with a quoted artifact (retro line, incident, missed milestone); ratings without evidence are not valid inputs.
rule.rationale: Unanchored RAG ratings default to Green due to politeness bias; evidence quotes force the assessor to confront actual data.

rule.statement: Re-score domain RAG monthly minimum AND on triggers: incident, scope change, leadership change.
rule.rationale: The value of the framework is the trend line, not the snapshot; trigger-based re-scoring catches mid-cycle regressions that monthly cadence misses.

list.item: Green: domain performing as expected, no gaps requiring action
Amber: domain has a documented gap with an active remediation plan
Red: domain has a critical gap with no remediation plan, or remediation failing

rule.statement: Assign one Accountable owner per domain; one person can hold multiple domains, but no domain can be unowned.
rule.rationale: Domains with no named owner drift to Green in status reports regardless of actual health; accountability produces honest reporting.

list.item: Governance → PM or PMO lead
Scope → PM or Product Owner
Schedule → PM
Finance → Sponsor or Finance lead
Stakeholders → PM (communications included)
Resources → Line Manager or PM
Risk → PM with risk register owner

section.name: cross-domain-interaction-chains
section.p: Domains are interdependent; a Red in one domain typically cascades to Amber or Red in others. Understanding these chains enables prioritized remediation.

list.item: Resources Red → Schedule Amber (capacity gap delays delivery)
Stakeholders Red → Finance Amber (sponsor disengagement delays approvals)
Governance Red → Scope Amber (unclear decision rights cause scope creep)
Risk Red → all domains (unmitigated risk materializes everywhere simultaneously)

rule.statement: When multiple domains are Red, identify which single domain improvement unblocks the most others; fix that first.
rule.rationale: Parallel remediation across all Red domains spreads PM attention too thin and produces no completed fixes; sequenced priority maximizes recovery velocity.

section.name: limitations-and-where-it-fails
section.p: PMBOK 8 domains are powerful but not comprehensive. Understand these limitations:

list.item: Brief / generic by design — agents need concrete prompts to make outputs actionable.
"Quality integrated everywhere" sounds nice but in practice means quality gets nobody's attention; teams must pin it to a domain owner.
Communications absorbed into Stakeholders means comms plans get under-invested; bring them back explicitly.
Procurement moved to appendix → vendors / contractors fall through the cracks unless an explicit owner is named.
"Sustainability" emphasis without local metrics is greenwashing; require domain checklists with measurable items.
Domains overlap (Resources ↔ Schedule ↔ Finance) — risk of double-counting work in dashboards.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A health-check agent ingests a project's docs (charter, plan, risk register, status reports) and produces a per-domain RAG status (Red/Amber/Green) with evidence quotes.
A planning agent generates a tailored "domain intensity" matrix per project — which domains need full treatment, which can be light.
A reviewer agent checks every status report or deliverable against the domain rubric and flags gaps (e.g., "no Stakeholders section in last 4 status reports").

list.item: domain-health-checker — scores each of the 7 domains on a project corpus and outputs RAG with citations.
tailoring-advisor — recommends which domains to apply heavily vs. lightly, given project type.
domain-rubric-linter — verifies status reports / deliverables cover all required domains.
value-delivery-coach — surfaces "output vs. outcome" misalignments (work shipped vs. value realized).

section.p: Prompt pattern: "You are domain-health-checker. Inputs: list of project documents (path + content). For each of the 7 domains (Governance, Scope, Schedule, Finance, Stakeholders, Resources, Risk), output: status (R/A/G), 2-3 evidence quotes with source path, top recommendation. Do not invent evidence. If a domain is not addressed in the corpus, say 'not addressed'."

list.item: Force every status report to have a section per domain — even one line each. Missing sections become the early warning.
Pair "Value Delivery" tracking with shipped output tracking; a 100% delivery rate with 30% value rate is the most common trap.
When tailoring, drop a domain with deliberate documentation; never silently. "We're not tracking Finance because budget is fixed annual" is fine; ignoring it is not.
Run a "domain owner" RACI: each of the 7 domains has one accountable person, even if the same person owns multiple.
For agile/hybrid programs, map ceremonies → domains (PI Planning → Schedule + Resources, Demos → Stakeholders + Value).
Re-score domain RAG monthly minimum; the value of the framework is the trend line, not the snapshot.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes when using agents with the PMBOK 8 domain framework:

list.item: Agents over-classify everything as Green if not given calibration examples; provide one explicit Red exemplar in the prompt.
"Quality" mentions tend to scatter across all 7 domains, making it invisible — explicitly ask the agent to surface quality-related findings as a cross-cutting section.
Sustainability gets hand-waved; require numeric metrics or "not applicable, justification: ..." with a reason.
LLMs invent PMBOK 7 vocabulary in PMBOK 8 contexts (and vice versa); pin the version in system prompt.
Stakeholder domain tempts agents into people-name name-dropping without consent — anonymize roles in shared outputs.
Long corpora cause domain conflation; chunk by domain (one inference per domain) for higher signal.

template.purpose: Per-project domain status manifest with owner, evidence, gap fields, and value-realization tracking.
